sn46.39:1.1: These trees are all “strangling figs”. They are proverbial in folklore for showing the threat of even the slightest evil (Ja 370, Ja 412). | E.J.H. Corner describes them in delicious detail in his Wayside Trees of Malaya (1940), vol. 1, pp. 664–5 (cited by Bodhi in his note on this discourse).
sn46.39:1.3: Kacchaka here is described as a parasitic strangler, which agrees with Bu Pc 11:2.1.6, where it is said to propagate from the trunk. It is sometimes identified with Toona ciliata, the “red cedar”, but that cannot be correct, as it is not a strangler. I translate as “Moreton bay fig” in homage to these magnificent trees that flourish throughout eastern Australia. | Kapitthana is Sanskrit kapītana, which Sanskrit sources identify with a bewildering variety of trees. One such, the Ficus virens or “white fig”, fits the description here.
